name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلنَّاسِ (١)
```

```english
Say, ‘I seek the protection of the Lord of humans, (1)
```

```arabic
مَلِكِ ٱلنَّاسِ (٢)
```

```english
Sovereign of humans, (2)
```

```arabic
إِلَـٰهِ ٱلنَّاسِ (٣)
```

```english
God of humans, (3)
```

```arabic
مِن شَرِّ ٱلْوَسْوَاسِ ٱلْخَنَّاسِ (٤)
```

```english
from the evil of the sneaky tempter (4)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى يُوَسْوِسُ فِى صُدُورِ ٱلنَّاسِ (٥)
```

```english
who puts temptations into the breasts of humans, (5)
```

```arabic
مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ (٦)
```

```english
from among the jinn and humans.’ (6)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to seek refuge (العوذ) with Allah from the evil of the retreating whisperer (الوسواس الخناس). This Surah is Medinan, like the one preceding it, as inferred from what has been narrated regarding the occasion of its revelation (شأن النزول); indeed, it is gathered from the narrations that the two Surahs were revealed together.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ النَّاسِ مَلِكِ النَّاسِ إِلهِ النَّاسِ** **Say, ‘I seek protection with the Lord of mankind, (1) the Sovereign of mankind, (2) the God of mankind, (3)** It is within the nature (طبع) of a human being, when faced with an evil they guard against and fear for themselves—feeling their own weakness—to seek refuge (الالتجاء) with one who has the power to repel it and suffice them against its occurrence. That which a person deems fit for seeking refuge (العوذ) and protection (الاعتصام) is one of three: either a Lord (رب) who oversees their affairs, manages them, and nurtures them, to whom they return for their general needs—and among the necessities for their survival is the repelling of that which threatens them with evil; this is a complete cause (سبب تام) in itself. Or, one who possesses great power and authority (سلطان), whose capability is far-reaching and whose decree is effective, who grants sanctuary (يجيره) if sought, thereby repelling evil through his authority, like a king among kings; this is also a complete and independent cause in itself.

There is a third cause, which is the worshipped God (الإله المعبود). For the necessity of the divinity (معبودية) of the God—especially if He is One without partner—is the servant’s sincere devotion (إخلاص) of themselves to Him. Thus, they do not call upon any but Him, nor do they return in any of their needs except to Him; they desire nothing but what He desires and act only according to His will.

Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of mankind (رب الناس), the Sovereign of mankind (ملك الناس), and the God of mankind (إله الناس), just as He combined these three attributes for Himself in His saying: **ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَأَنَّى تُصْرَفُونَ** **That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs the kingdom; there is no god except Him; so where are you being led away?** (39:6). He, the Exalted, pointed to the causality (سببية) of His Lordship and Divinity in His saying: **رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَاتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلاً** **The Lord of the east and the west, there is no god except Him, so take Him for a trustee.** (73:9), and to the causality of His Sovereignty in His saying: **لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَإِلَى اللهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ** **To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah are all matters returned.** (57:5). Thus, if a human seeks refuge from a threatening evil with a Lord, then Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord, and there is no Lord besides Him. If they desire for their refuge a Sovereign, then Allah, the Glorified, is the True Sovereign; to Him belongs the kingdom and to Him belongs the judgment (١). And if they desire for that a God, then He is the God, and there is no god other than Him.

**Thus, His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ النَّاسِ** (Say, ‘I seek protection with the Lord of mankind) etc., is a command to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to seek refuge with Him because he is among mankind, and He, the Exalted, is the Lord of mankind, the Sovereign of mankind, and the God of mankind.

From what has preceded, the following becomes clear: First, the reason for specifying these three attributes—the Lord (الرب), the Sovereign (الملك), and the God (الإله)—from among all His other noble attributes, as well as the reason for the order between them. The Lord was mentioned first because He is closest to the human being and possesses the most specific guardianship (ولاية). Then the Sovereign, because he is further in reach and possesses a more general guardianship, sought by one who has no specific guardian to suffice them. Then the God, because He is the Guardian sought by the human being out of their sincerity (إخلاص), not merely out of their material nature.

Second, the reason for not connecting His saying: **مَلِكِ النَّاسِ إِلهِ النَّاسِ** (the Sovereign of mankind, the God of mankind) with a conjunction (العطف). This is to indicate that each of these attributes is an independent cause (سبب مستقل) in repelling evil. He, the Exalted, is an independent cause by virtue of being a Lord, by virtue of being a Sovereign, and by virtue of being a God. Thus, causality (السببية) belongs to Him in whatever sense a cause is intended. A similar reasoning was previously mentioned regarding His saying: **اللهُ أَحَدٌ اللهُ الصَّمَدُ** (Allah is One, Allah is the All-embracing).

By this, the reason for repeating the word "mankind" (الناس) instead of saying "their Lord and their God" also becomes apparent. It points to the fact that each of the three attributes can be the object of seeking refuge on its own without mentioning the others, due to its independence; and to Allah belong all the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى). Other commentators have offered various explanations for the specification of these attributes and other aforementioned details, but they are of little avail.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مِنْ شَرِّ الْوَسْواسِ الْخَنَّاسِ** **from the evil of the sneaking whisperer, (4)** It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "The whisper (الوسواس) is the inner speech of the soul (حديث النفس) with that which is like a hidden sound." Thus, it is a verbal noun (مصدر) like *al-waswasah* (الوسوسة), as he mentioned. They noted that its usage as such is based on oral tradition (سماعي), whereas the analogical form (القياس) for verbal nouns of the four-radical root (الرباعي المجرد) is with a *kasrah* on the first letter. In any case, it appears—as has been inferred—that what is intended here is the adjectival meaning (المعنى الوصفي) for the sake of emphasis (مبالغة). Some have said it is an adjective (صفة) and not a verbal noun.

The "retreating one" (الخناس) is an intensive active participle (صيغة مبالغة) from *al-khunus* (الخنوس), meaning to disappear after appearing. It is said: The Shaytan is called *khannas* because he whispers to the human being, but when Allah, the Exalted, is remembered, he retreats and falls back; then, when the person becomes heedless, he returns to his whispering.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِي يُوَسْوِسُ فِي صُدُورِ النَّاسِ** **who whispers in the breasts of mankind, (5)** This is an attribute of the retreating whisperer. What is meant by "breasts" (الصدور) is the souls (النفوس), because the locus of whispering is the principle of perception (مبدأ الإدراك) in the human being, which is their soul. The breasts were taken as the place for whispering only because perception is attributed, according to common usage, to the heart (القلب), and the heart is in the breast, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَلكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ** **but it is the hearts which are in the breasts that go blind.** (22:46).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَالنَّاسِ** **from among the jinn and mankind.’ (6)** This is an exposition (بيان) of the retreating whisperer. It contains an indication that among mankind are those who are joined with the devils (الشياطين) and are in their ranks, as He, the Exalted, said: **شَياطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَالْجِنِّ** **the devils from among men and jinn** (6:112).

## (Narrative Discourse)

In *al-Majma‘*: Abu Khadijah narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام who said: "Gabriel came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was unwell, and he treated him with the two Surahs of seeking refuge (المعوذتين) and 'Say, He is Allah, the One.' He said: **بسم الله أرقيك والله يشفيك من كل داء يؤذيك ـ خذها فلتهنيك** — 'In the Name of Allah I treat you, and Allah heals you from every ailment that harms you; take it and may it be a blessing for you.' Then he said: **بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم ـ قل أعوذ برب الناس** — 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. Say, I seek protection with the Lord of mankind...' to the end of the Surah."

**I say:** Some narrations regarding the occasion of the Surah's revelation have been mentioned previously.

In the same source, it is narrated that Anas ibn Malik said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **إن الشيطان واضع خطمه على قلب ابن آدم ـ فإذا ذكر الله خنس وإذا نسي التقم ـ فذلك الوسواس الخناس** — "Indeed, the Shaytan places his snout upon the heart of the son of Adam; when he remembers Allah, he retreats (خنس), and when he forgets, he swallows [the heart]; that is the retreating whisperer (الوسواس الخناس)."

In the same source, al-‘Ayyashi narrated with his chain from Aban ibn Taghlib from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad عليه‌السلام who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **ما من مؤمن إلا ولقلبه في صدره أذنان ـ أذن ينفث فيها الملك وأذن ينفث فيها الوسواس الخناس ـ فيؤيد الله المؤمن بالملك ، وهو قوله سبحانه : « وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ »** — "There is no believer except that his heart in his breast has two ears: an ear into which the angel breathes, and an ear into which the retreating whisperer breathes. Thus, Allah supports the believer with the angel, and that is His saying, the Glorified: **وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ** (and He supported them with a spirit from Him)."

In *Amali al-Saduq*, with his chain to al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, he said: "When this verse was revealed: **وَالَّذِينَ إِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً ـ أَوْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا اللهَ فَاسْتَغْفَرُوا لِذُنُوبِهِمْ** (and those who, when they commit an indecency or wrong themselves, remember Allah and plead for forgiveness for their sins) [3:135], Iblis climbed a mountain in Mecca called Thuwair. He cried out at the top of his voice to his demons (عفاريته), and they gathered to him. They said: 'O our master, why have you called us?' He said: 'This verse has been revealed; who is up for it?' A demon from among the devils stood up and said: 'I am for it by doing such and such.' He said: 'You are not for it.' Another stood up and said the like, and he said: 'You are not for it.'

Then the retreating whisperer (الوسواس الخناس) said: 'I am for it.' He asked: 'By what means?' He replied: 'I will promise them and arouse desires in them until they fall into sin; then, when they fall into sin, I will make them forget to seek forgiveness.' He said: 'You are for it,' and he put him in charge of it until the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** Some discussion regarding the Shaytan preceded in the beginning of the eighth volume of this book.

* * *

The book is completed, and praise be to Allah. Its composition was finished on the blessed Night of Power (ليلة القدر), the twenty-third of the nights of the month of Ramadan, in the year one thousand three hundred and ninety-two after the Hijrah. Praise be to Allah forever, and peace and blessings be upon our master Muhammad and his Progeny.

__________________
(1) *al-Taghabun*: 1.
